text: Oh, Marvel, you could not live with your own failure. And where did that bring you? Back to me. In case you've been living under a rock for the past couple of weeks or just passed out drunk from too much festive celebration, you're probably going to know that Marvel have started dropping teaser trailers for Avengers Doomsday, which is still like a year away, but what the hell, you've got to build that hype up somehow. And hats off to them for this one. Using Fire and

Ass, probably the biggest Disney movie of the entire year, as an exclusive release mechanism was a pretty genius marketing move. Whoever came up with that idea, give that man a raise. >> No, no, I don't think I will. >> Right from the get-go, Marvel have made no secret of the fact that Doomsday was going to be all about one thing, nostalgia. arguably because that's the only card left for the MCU to play at this point before it all gets rebooted and the cycle of money-making begins

again with a new generation. And yeah, it might be cheap and manipulative, but you've got to admit it's pretty [ __ ] effective. Remember all those characters you used to love in other better movies when our franchise was actually still good? Well, they're back for one last adventure. This is the last time you're ever going to see them on the big screen. You don't want to miss out on that, do you? You dick. It's a bit like that cool new band you used to love as a

teenager, but then they aged out of their style and their last few albums were kind of [ __ ] and you moved on to other stuff and gradually lost interest. But then you find out they're playing one last farewell gig in your hometown. You're probably going to go see them for old times sake if nothing else. And because the entire movie is predicated on which nostalgia bait characters and actors they're able to get back, the teasers they've released so far have

reflected that. The first one focused on the return of Steve Rogers for the first time in six years. Probably the worstkept secret in movie making. I guess Captain Falcon Man wasn't the big hit they hoped for. A >> he's out of line, but he's right. >> It's almost like a character is more than just the clothes they wear or the shield they carry and copy pasting them onto someone else doesn't transfer the emotional investment you had in the original. Especially when the new guy is

used as a cheap excuse to lecture you about how domestic terrorism is actually good and everyone needs to do better without providing any actual ideas or solutions of his own. >> But you have no idea how complicated this situation is. >> You know what? You're right. And that's a good thing. >> You sure about that? You sure about that? >> Let's be honest here. We all knew Chris Evans was going to come back to the MCU at some point. You can only say no to those big Marvel paychecks and all that

media exposure for so long. And well, it's not like he's been drowning in blockbuster movie success since Endgame. The teaser depicts him as a loving father reluctantly called back to the fight to help save the worlds. >> What the [ __ ] did I just hear? >> Wait a second. A capable, loving, successful, aspirational father figure in a Disney movie. >> Are you out of your goddamn mind? >> What kind of bizarro world are we living in here? Next, you'll be telling me that

Thor isn't portrayed as a goofy joke. >> So that I may fight once more, defeat one more enemy, and return home to her. >> Oh my god, Taoiti must be spinning in his grave right now. Wait, what do you mean he's not dead? Oh, sorry. It's just his career that's dead. You know, as much as the first two Thor movies took themselves a little too seriously and hadn't quite figured out how to use the character properly, the more I think about it, the more I realize I'd rather watch their earnest

but clumsy attempts to live up to the Shakespearean grandeur of the god of thunder than ever subject myself to farcical, self-deprecating sludge like Love and Thunder. Say what you want about them, but there's a kind of simplistic sincerity and honesty to their take on Thor instead of making him the butt of endless jokes and cheap ironic deconstruction. In fact, I do believe I speak for the entire world when I say we are so [ __ ] done with having our heroes and our stories and

our mythologies deconstructed and mocked. They've been deconstructed to the point where there's literally nothing left of them. And for what? so that some wanky, pretentious video essays can jerk off to the ironic postmodernist reflection of today's nihilistic, morally gray world. Son of a [ __ ] [ __ ] off, wanky, pretentious video essaists. Sometimes it's okay for a hero to just be a [ __ ] hero. Anyway, I digress. Much like the Steve Rogers teaser, Thor is also depicted as

the archetype of a father living a peaceful life, forced to take up arms once more to defend everything he loves. It's a surprisingly honest, heartfelt, sincere, dramatic scene that just works. No Valkyrie with her [ __ ] eatating smirk to put him down. No [ __ ] rock monster making quippy observations about everything that's happening. No Jane Foster trying to outdo him like a walking inferiority complex. Just a man, older and wiser and scarred by past battles, looking deep inside for the

strength to go into the fray one more time. The third teaser, which I can't show yet because it hasn't been released on YouTube and I don't want to get sued, focuses on Professor X and Magneto agreeing to work together. Two old friends and enemies with a lifetime of baggage and conflicting ideologies behind them, learning to put aside their differences and work together for the greater goods. Again, it's simple but effective stuff. No shitty jokes, no ironic quips, no girl bosses to

undermine the moment. Just two people we know and care about having a scene together that's actually allowed to breathe and sink in. >> No way. Yes way. >> Jesus H Christ. Is Marvel actually growing up? Have they actually listened to all the fan complaints and the box office bombs and taken steps to correct it? I don't know. These are just teasers at the end of the day. For all I know, the finished movie could still be a chaotic goofy self-deprecating disaster that makes Love and Thunder

look like war and peace. But if these teasers are in any way reflective of the end product, well, I think Marvel are playing the best hand they possibly can here. They're making smart moves, treating Doomsday with the gravitas and seriousness it deserves, putting their most iconic heroes that they know people actually care about front and center instead of a bunch of astroturf diversity hires and cheap carbon copies. You know, it's amazing what you can do when you give your customer what they

actually want instead of force-feeding them what you think they need. But you can't please everyone all of the time. And the people who make it their business to be offended on behalf of the entire human race have already started complaining about the lack of diversity in the teasers. >> Oh no. What? What happened? >> Girls get it done. >> No you don't, sweetheart. That's the problem. Where are the Captain Marvels? The She-Hulks, the Iron Harts, the Captain Falcon Man, the Eternals.

Damn. Was that actually a real movie? Well, dear viewer, the problem with all those characters is nobody gives a [ __ ] about them. They never did and they never will. And after half a decade of practically ramming them down our throats and demanding we accept them, I think even Marvel are starting to realize this [ __ ] ain't working, man. They've practically destroyed their own brand and driven away their core audience trying to appease the activists who never gave a [ __ ] about their

product in the first place and push the message that's been rejected time and time again and ultimately something had to give. Either they changed course and tried to undo the damage they've inflicted on themselves or they go out of business. [ __ ] pick one. In fact, from what I've been told, Kevin Feige himself privately admitted defeat on all this stuff a couple of years ago when he said, "We've done our bit for king and country. Now we need to get back to

making money again." Huh. It's a crazy plan, but it just might work. It's funny how quickly all that nonsense goes out the window when actual survival becomes the priority again, isn't it? It's a bit like when there's another foreign war brewing and you start seeing all male recruitment ads for the army. That's when you realize, "Oh [ __ ] they want actual soldiers again." Now, these are all necessary steps to get the MCU back to something vaguely resembling long-term profitability again. But in

the short term, it does leave them in the tricky position of having pissed off just about everyone. The modern audience they've been desperately courting for the past 5 years are going to be so deeply triggered by the swerve back towards traditional male heroes that there's not enough chamomile tea in the world to calm them down. While the core audience that made them a global brand in the first place have been burned and driven away by years of self-righteous

lectures and tedious identity politics, a lot of them have moved on to other things now, and they might prove harder to win back than Marvel think, especially if they plan to reboot and relaunch the whole MCU after Secret Wars. You're going to need a large and dedicated fan base to stick with you through a major shakeup like that. And I don't know if Marvel can count on it anymore. But hey, it's going to be fun to watch them try. Anyway, that's all I've got for today. Go away now.
